items.q: Is sodium metal produced at the cathode when brine is electrolysed?
items.correct: No — hydrogen is discharged instead because it is less reactive than sodium; the sodium ions stay in solution.
items.misconception: Electrolysis of sodium chloride solution deposits sodium metal at the cathode.
items.random_wrong: Electrolysis of brine gives oxygen at the cathode.
items.why: Students apply the molten-salt result to the aqueous case, forgetting that water provides hydrogen ions that are discharged in preference.

items.q: Do electrons flow through the electrolyte?
items.correct: No — moving ions carry the charge through the electrolyte; electrons flow only through the external wires and electrodes.
items.misconception: Electrons travel through the solution from one electrode to the other.
items.random_wrong: Protons carry the current through the metal wires.
items.why: Students picture one continuous electron loop; the circuit actually switches charge carrier from electrons in metal to ions in solution.

items.q: Are positive ions oxidised at the cathode?
items.correct: No — positive ions gain electrons at the cathode, which is reduction.
items.misconception: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode because they change into atoms.
items.random_wrong: No reactions happen at the cathode; everything occurs at the anode.
items.why: Students attach 'oxidation' to any change at an electrode; OIL RIG fixes the direction — gaining electrons is reduction.

items.q: During electrolysis, positive ions move towards the:
items.why: It's Cathode. “Anode” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Electrolysis of molten lead(II) bromide produces at the anode:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bromine. “Lead” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Why must an ionic compound be molten or dissolved before it can be electrolysed?
items.correct: Its ions become free to move and carry the charge
items.random_wrong: The solid would melt the electrodes
items.why: Correct answer: Its ions become free to move and carry the charge. “Its electrons become delocalised” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: At the cathode during electrolysis of concentrated brine:
items.why: It's Hydrogen gas is produced. “Sodium metal is produced” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
